text: Hello everyone, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California. This is a case of a midline incisional hernia repair with a ileal conduit on the right side present.
The surgeon uses an ETEP approach with ports placed in the left abdomen, dissecting around the visible stoma without encountering a peristomal hernia.
The challenge in this case was ensuring that I had good mesh overlap near the area of the stoma to ensure that the midline incisional hernia did not come back.

The surgeon completes dissection around the stoma, revealing the right retrorectus space. Measurements are taken, showing a four-centimeter distance between the right edge of the defect and the stoma. The midline defect measures approximately eight by eleven centimeters, indicating a single large hernia.
The camera focuses on the ileal conduit, which is visible on the right side of the surgical field.

The surgeon sutures the anterior defect, leaving a veil of the posterior rectus sheath visible. Approximately one centimeter of the posterior rectus sheath is preserved to prevent retraction of the anterior rectus sheath, aiding in identifying the fascia.

The surgeon uses a 24-inch OV lock suture to progressively tension the hernia defect together, taking small bites of the hernia sac to reduce the size of the seroma and create a multi-loculated seroma.

The surgeon decreases the pressure to facilitate re-approximation of the defect, carefully peeling back muscle to expose the fascia. Solid bites are taken to encircle the linea alba, progressively tensioning the defect together for closure.
The surgeon completes the closure of the defect, leaving approximately five centimeters of overlap cephalad. The suture is being run back to secure the closure.
Next I'm going to close the posterior layer which I'm not showing on this video since the focus is mainly just regarding how I manage this aileron conduit.
The surgeon places a 19 by 30 centimeter parietine mesh into the pelvic region, ensuring it covers the necessary area for reinforcement.
The surgeon performs a lower right one-third transversus abdominis release (TAR) to assist in closing a large posterior gap, with the camera view oriented cephalad.
The surgeon presses the large parietine mesh against the stoma, preparing to slit the mesh and wrap it around the stoma for secure placement.

The surgeon positions the mesh to achieve more than four centimeters of overlap cephalad and caudal to the stoma, with approximately four centimeters at the stoma itself. Due to insufficient overlap, the mesh is sutured down to the posterior rectus sheath for secure placement.
The surgeon tucks the mesh beyond the transversus abdominis release (TAR) point and uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to fixate the mesh at multiple points, ensuring stability post-operation.
With this amount of overlap, I doubt that there would be much shift either way, but this just makes me sleep a little better. And it makes me less worried about future recurrence.

In this case, I really didn't have good other options. Going intra-abdominal with an IPOM was an option. Pre-partenial would have been very difficult. Certainly open would have given me the same mesh overlap results, but it would be more invasive.
An onlay, in my opinion, is not a good option in this case, although you could create a subcutaneous pocket around the estoma and lay an onlay mesh similar to this.
The surgeon places a 19 French round JP drain near the stoma site at the end of the procedure, ensuring drainage post-operation.
